speaker: interviewer
text: I'm conducting an interview with Odette Ross in London, England, and this is tape number four.

speaker: interviewee
text: When you came for your first interview, you asked me whether I had a message or what I've perhaps-- maybe have learned. I think what I do know, that I value and cherish freedom, any sort of freedom, anyway, more than anything in the world. And I have these tremendous arguments with my husband's South African relatives, who feel that they can't give this up and can't give that up. My regret, particularly in view of this interview, is that I could never even talk to my parents about it, or my parents to-- to-- to me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My brother and I share this experience in silence, and we never shared anguish or fright because as I said before, we seemed to just be existing until we survived, and there was no point in feeling sorry for ourselves or discussing this sort of thing. My brother and my father are dead, and my mother, who is well over 90, is in a world of her own, so I couldn't get any more details from her. My daughter says I will never speak. I didn't-- I couldn't speak about my experiences. And I firmly believe that my children didn't really want to hear. I know that my grandchildren are very keen, and it is really for them that I've made this testimony. And I'm very grateful to the Shoah Foundation and to Steven Spielberg to give me the opportunity of doing this. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your children ever regret not having grandparents? I mean, do they have grandparents on your husband's side? Does your husband have--

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- I had no-- my children have grandchildren-- grandparents. I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, right. You didn't have grand-- sorry. Yeah. Did they have grandparents from your husband? Did your husband's parents--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they did have--

speaker: interviewee
text: My children had grandparents. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm very conscious. I have, I think, a very good relationship with the grandchildren, and I think that grandparents are very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of relationship did your children have with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's something you'd have to ask them. I don't know. We saw a lot of them. They certainly saw their father's parents nearly every Friday. My parents returned to Belgium. But I think there was a warm feeling because my son went to visit my mother two months ago, and I warned him that she probably wouldn't know him as he's absolutely white now. And she didn't know him, and I think he was very upset. Yes, I think that the extended family is very important. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Are we still on? Do you want to take photographs?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. I think this is a good time to call your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is Arnold, my husband of 48 years, and we've been together ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think there's anything that reflects in your relationship or-- or may have made your life different if Odette hadn't gone through all that she went through during the war years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think it's played a very big influence on her life in her later years. And as the years go by, she seems to be more involved in the years she spent under the Germans and the escaping, I think maybe because our grandchildren are so interested in it, it's all come back to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, psychologically, it's a well-known thing that whilst you're young and active and work, you try to obliterate it, and as you get old and retired and have more time, the-- the-- real survivors sometimes suffer depression at this stage of their life because of memories coming back, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you know, you-- you recognize-- you recognize that she's more involved with that now than she was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do, very much so, yes. Yes. Any new book that comes out on the subject, she's anxious to read, and also plays or films about the subject.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's only because I feel that the world must not forget. I think it's terribly, terribly important that those who are alive should tell the others because it's a tragedy and a Holocaust that's never been heard of before.

speaker: interviewer
text: And hopefully, never again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so great that people find it too large to absorb.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, perhaps less now than when it-- in the earlier years just after the war. People seem to be able to relax and talk about it more than they used to just after the war, I find.

speaker: interviewee
text: This Passover, I thought the time had come to tell the grandchildren a little bit about it, and I told them about having lost my grandparents. I also mentioned the million and a half children who were killed. Now, that was just too large for them to comprehend, but when I told them that I lost 17 cousins, and they're very much into cousins, this really struck a note. I think the tragedy was just too overwhelming for people to believe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I agree.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are the grandchildren that are asking you lots of questions? Are they-- they quite young?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, from-- the youngest is nine, and the eldest is 18. And I think the two younger ones are more interested. But then, they live--

speaker: interviewer
text: Closer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Locally. They live in-- in-- in London, and they mix with all sorts of communities, but they mix with quite a few Jews, whereas my son lives in a purely academic--

speaker: interviewer
text: Environment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Environment and I think that besides science and physics, they don't talk about much else, perhaps football.

speaker: interviewer
text: But do you think that the younger ones are more open? If they've got a question, they'll ask you and not worry about whether they might upset you, whereas the older ones might be a bit more--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and also I think because they are the children of a daughter. I think there is a different approach in children from a daughter than there are children from a man. They say-- what is the saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-- a-- a-- a son is a son until he gets a wife. A daughter is a daughter for the rest of her life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think this is true, yes. This is my paternal grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Fromit Rubenstein, nee Infeld, I-N-F-E-L-D. I think this photograph must have been taken just before she was taken away because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she was in her 60s when she was deported, and that photograph looks as if she could be about that age. Unfortunately, I haven't got one of her-- a photograph of her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us who's in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of my parents taken in England in 1941, just about a year before we were reunited. This is my mother's eldest sister, who is 102 today. She was 90 on that photograph. She's had a remarkable, sad, and interesting and fascinating life. And unfortunately, she lost her only daughter in a concentration camp in 1942, and this is a photograph of her daughter, Remonde.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is her daughter, Remonde, age 17. She died when she was about 18 or 19. This is my brother, Lucien, at the age of 17, soon after we arrived in London in 1942. This is a photograph of myself at college in Cambridge in 1945. I'm in the front row, third from the left. This is my husband and I on our wedding day, leaving [INAUDIBLE] Synagogue on the 31st of March, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a-- a picture of my parents as I like to remember them. They were back in Belgium at that time. I think my mother must have been about 60, and my father about 66, 67. This is one of the last family photographs we have. We try and take one once a year. This was April, 1995. Top row is my daughter, Denise, and my son-in-law, Roger, my daughter-in-law, Jackie, my son, Douglas, myself. Bottom row, left-- Arnold, my grand-- my son's youngest son, Alex, my daughter's eldest son, Matthew, my daughter's younger son, Thomas, and my son's eldest son, Julius. This is an entry visa to Bolivia, which my uncle and aunt purchased in Antwerp in 1941, hoping that they would then get transferred visa to enable to go to Bolivia, although that was not really their destination. The photograph shows at the top my uncle, with whom I lived, my aunt, with whom I lived, and my brother. There are no photographs of my two young cousins or myself because we were too young and didn't need any papers at the time to travel as minors.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said your aunts and uncle purchased this visa. Do you know how much it cost them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea, but I know that the South American consuls were all bribable, and most Jews, as some form of security, originally thought that if they had an exit visa-- an entry visa to somewhere, they would be able to exit some time. It helped some and didn't help others.
